fact: Catania had a population of around twenty thousand at the time of the 1669 eruption
source: Widely documented; referenced in 'Cities on Vesuvius' and various historical accounts of 17th-century Sicilian demographics. The Royal Society's contemporary account by Lord Winchilsea describes Catania as a major Sicilian city.

fact: Mount Etna opened a fissure approximately a mile long during the 1669 eruption
source: The eruption began on March 11, 1669, with a large fissure opening on Etna's southern flank. Described in the Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society, based on Lord Winchilsea's eyewitness account (1669).

fact: Residents built stone barriers to divert the lava, and people from neighboring areas (Paternò) tore the barriers down to protect their own homes
source: This is one of the earliest recorded attempts at lava diversion. Documented in contemporary accounts and discussed in 'Volcanoes of Europe' by Jerram & Scarth (2020) and in the Smithsonian Global Volcanism Program entry for Etna.

fact: The medieval city walls of Catania held back the lava flow initially but caused it to pool and rise, eventually overtopping or breaching the walls
source: Described in Lord Winchilsea's letter to the Royal Society (1669) and in 'Etna: A History of the Mountain and Its Eruptions' by G. F. Rodwell (1878).

fact: Lava buried roughly half of Catania, with the city walls acting as a dam that held molten rock against the city
source: The western part of Catania was destroyed and buried under lava. Documented in Boris Behncke's geological studies of Etna and in the INGV (Istituto Nazionale di Geofisica e Vulcanologia) historical records.
